title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a woman called Amelia Hart, who runs a cookery school.
audio_script: Interviewer: Today we're speaking to Amelia Hart, who runs a small cookery school in Bath. Amelia, did you always want to teach cooking? || Guest: Not really. My mum was a chef, and people imagine I copied her, but I was too shy to work in a restaurant. I studied business first. [20]I only started teaching after a friend asked me to show some students how to make simple meals, and I enjoyed explaining each step[/20]. It felt more natural than cooking for customers. || Interviewer: What do beginners usually find hardest? || Guest: They often worry about knives or timing, but those things improve quickly. [21]The real problem is that many people try to follow a recipe without reading it through first[/21]. Then they suddenly discover something has to rest for an hour, or the oven should already be hot. I tell them to sit down with a cup of tea and read before touching any food. || Interviewer: What kind of class is your favourite to teach? || Guest: I like the family classes, though they can be noisy. [22]What I enjoy most is teaching adults who think they are hopeless, because they are so pleased when something turns out well[/22]. They usually arrive saying they can only boil pasta, and by the end they have made soup, bread and a pudding. Their confidence changes during the day. || Interviewer: Has anything about your work surprised you? || Guest: Yes, the amount of planning. People see the fun part, but [23]I spend far more time checking ingredients, testing recipes and preparing equipment than I expected[/23]. If one small thing is missing, the whole class can go badly. I used to leave too much until the morning, but now I prepare everything the day before. || Interviewer: You also make short cooking videos. How do you feel about them? || Guest: At first I hated being filmed. I still don't like watching myself, but [24]the videos have been useful because people practise at home before coming to a class[/24]. It means they are less nervous and ask better questions. I don't want to become famous online; I just want the videos to help learners. || Interviewer: What advice would you give someone who wants to cook better? || Guest: Don't buy lots of expensive equipment. [25]Choose three or four meals you really like and practise them several times[/25]. When you can make those without panic, add something new. People often jump from one recipe to another, but repeating the same dish teaches you more than trying something difficult once. || Interviewer: Do students usually continue cooking after the course? || Guest: Many do, especially if they have come with a friend or family member. I send everyone home with a short list of ingredients and a plan for the next week. I don't expect them to make perfect meals immediately. What matters is that they cook again while the lesson is still fresh in their minds. Some people send me photos later, which is always lovely. A few have even returned with their teenagers, because they want cooking to become a normal activity at home rather than something stressful. I also remind students that mistakes are useful, because burned onions or flat bread tell you something. A relaxed kitchen is a better place to learn than a perfect one.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Today we're speaking to Amelia Hart, who runs a small cookery school in Bath. Amelia, did you always want to teach cooking?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Not really. My mum was a chef, and people imagine I copied her, but I was too shy to work in a restaurant. I studied business first. I only started teaching after a friend asked me to show some students how to make simple meals, and I enjoyed explaining each step. It felt more natural than cooking for customers.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do beginners usually find hardest?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: They often worry about knives or timing, but those things improve quickly. The real problem is that many people try to follow a recipe without reading it through first. Then they suddenly discover something has to rest for an hour, or the oven should already be hot. I tell them to sit down with a cup of tea and read before touching any food.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What kind of class is your favourite to teach?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I like the family classes, though they can be noisy. What I enjoy most is teaching adults who think they are hopeless, because they are so pleased when something turns out well. They usually arrive saying they can only boil pasta, and by the end they have made soup, bread and a pudding. Their confidence changes during the day.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Has anything about your work surprised you?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes, the amount of planning. People see the fun part, but I spend far more time checking ingredients, testing recipes and preparing equipment than I expected. If one small thing is missing, the whole class can go badly. I used to leave too much until the morning, but now I prepare everything the day before.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: You also make short cooking videos. How do you feel about them?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: At first I hated being filmed. I still don't like watching myself, but the videos have been useful because people practise at home before coming to a class. It means they are less nervous and ask better questions. I don't want to become famous online; I just want the videos to help learners.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What advice would you give someone who wants to cook better?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Don't buy lots of expensive equipment. Choose three or four meals you really like and practise them several times. When you can make those without panic, add something new. People often jump from one recipe to another, but repeating the same dish teaches you more than trying something difficult once.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do students usually continue cooking after the course?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Many do, especially if they have come with a friend or family member. I send everyone home with a short list of ingredients and a plan for the next week. I don't expect them to make perfect meals immediately. What matters is that they cook again while the lesson is still fresh in their minds. Some people send me photos later, which is always lovely. A few have even returned with their teenagers, because they want cooking to become a normal activity at home rather than something stressful. I also remind students that mistakes are useful, because burned onions or flat bread tell you something. A relaxed kitchen is a better place to learn than a perfect one.

questions.question: Why did Amelia first start teaching cooking?

options.A: A friend asked her to help some learners.
options.B: She wanted to work like her mother.
options.C: She needed to earn money while studying.

questions.explanation: Amelia says she started teaching after a friend asked her to show students how to make simple meals.

questions.question: What does Amelia say beginners often do wrong?

options.B: They do not read the recipe carefully first.
options.C: They cook everything for too long.

questions.explanation: She says the real problem is that people follow a recipe without reading it through first.

questions.question: What type of learners does Amelia most enjoy teaching?

options.B: children who are confident in the kitchen
options.C: adults who believe they cannot cook well

questions.explanation: She enjoys teaching adults who think they are hopeless because they are pleased when they succeed.

questions.question: What surprised Amelia about running classes?
questions.explanation: She says she spends far more time preparing ingredients and equipment than she expected.

questions.question: How have Amelia's videos helped her students?

options.A: They have made the school better known.
options.B: They allow students to practise before class.
options.C: They show students difficult restaurant dishes.

questions.explanation: She says the videos help people practise at home before coming to class.

questions.question: What does Amelia advise people to do?

options.A: buy a few useful kitchen tools
options.B: try new recipes as often as possible
options.C: repeat a small number of favourite meals

questions.explanation: Amelia advises choosing three or four meals and practising them several times.
